one_line_takeaway: A single-day, human-imperceptible headline edit (Cyrillic homoglyph or hidden HTML clause) can silently cut an LLM-driven trading system's annual returns by up to 17.7 percentage points, and the most profitable finance-tuned LLMs are the most vulnerable while the only robust one (O3) trades profit for safety.
key_finding: Both imperceptible attacks reliably mislead the LLM and translate into measurable portfolio losses despite the ATS still closing at a profit (making the attack undetectable to owners). On FinBERT, the homoglyph attack breaks stock-name association for 99.1% of edited headlines (5,957 of 6,012) and causes a decision change on 100% of attacked days, with mean delta-CR of -3.67 pp and a worst case of -17.70 pp (TSLA, 28 Feb 2024). The hidden-text attack flips sentiment polarity on 65.6% of headlines, reduces CR in 77.3% of trials (mean -3.18 pp, worst -17.7 pp on the same TSLA/28-Feb day). Transferability holds across almost all 9 additional LLMs. A key trade-off emerges: the only robust model, OpenAI O3 (only 8.32 pp homoglyph mapping drop, ~0.5% CR impact, 91% of days unaffected because it recognizes/sanitizes Cyrillic), is NOT the most profitable on clean news, whereas the most profitable clean models (FinLLaMA 19.5% CR, FinGPT) are among the most vulnerable. Defenses are hard because the loss is a hidden counterfactual margin, not an availability disruption.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first system-wide, monetarily-quantified security evaluation of 'adversarial news' attacks against LLM-driven algorithmic trading systems (ATS). The authors build a realistic ATS in Backtrader that fuses an LSTM price forecast with LLM-derived headline sentiment, then measure how two human-imperceptible headline manipulations propagate into portfolio losses. The two attacks are: (1) Unicode homoglyph substitution (replacing Latin characters in a stock name with visually identical Cyrillic ones) to break the LLM's stock-name/ticker association, and (2) hidden-text injection (a sentiment-reversing clause wrapped in <span style="display:none">) to flip the LLM's sentiment score. The novelty over prior adversarial-ML-in-finance work (which targeted only LSTM/price inputs, e.g., reference [21], or studied text perturbations at the model level only) is the end-to-end quantification of dollar impact on a full LLM-driven ATS. Note: numbers such as FinMA-7B's 88% sentiment accuracy vs GPT-4's 78%, and the Transformer-vs-LSTM RMSE improvements from Yanez et al., appear in the Related Work section and are NOT this paper's own results.
threat_model: Attacker is OUTSIDE the ATS with no direct access to its internals (no knowledge of model parameters, training data, full portfolio, or price feeds). The adversary knows only that the ATS ingests financial-news headlines, maps them to tickers, and derives sentiment (a common, publicly-documented practice), and knows at least ONE stock traded by the ATS (e.g., GOOGL, inferred from prior trades). Capability: can alter headlines mentioning that one known stock, either by authoring adversarial news at the source, via a man-in-the-middle compromise of the publishing pipeline, or (unlikely) at the vendor. Constraint: manipulation must be human-imperceptible and can be applied on AT MOST ONE DAY across the 14-month window; the attacker cannot touch prices, retrain models, alter anything beyond the headline, or persist across days. Goal: reduce the ATS owner's cumulative returns (a stealthy loss, since the ATS still turns a profit and owners stay oblivious).
limitations: The authors evaluate one specific ATS configuration (fixed trading frequency, historical window, broker, models, portfolio) and explicitly do NOT claim generality of results. Real-world ATS may behave differently, and the LSTM+LLM fusion component had to be built from scratch because reference [9]'s code is not public. Refinitiv news data cannot be released (proprietary), limiting exact reproducibility (though reimplementation with public feeds like Tiingo is described). The evaluation restricts the attacker to a single day and a single known stock; a real attacker could combine both attacks, use other homoglyph variants (e.g., pipe '|' for 'l') or other hidden-text tricks (font-size:0pt, background-color text) that the proof-of-concept sanitizer does not cover. The user study (n=27) is convenience-sampled and the authors refrain from generalizable claims from it. No IRB was mandated by their institution.

datasets_used: Yahoo Finance daily OHLCV bars (2013-2025) for 10 large-cap US equities {GOOGL, AAPL, NVDA, MSFT, AMZN, META, TSLA, LLY, JPM, XOM}
Refinitiv historical financial-news headlines (proprietary; ~6,012 headlines in the attack evaluation, ~7,000 manually verified for stock-name association and sentiment)
Original user-study responses from 27 FinTech practitioners (Sept 2025, 32% response rate)
Systematic literature review corpus of ~25k papers (2,169 security-venue 2024-2025 + 23,038 ML-venue 2020-2025)
quantitative_results: Baseline cumulative return: LSTM-only ATS 7.9%; LSTM+LLM (FinBERT) 19.22% over the 14-month window

Homoglyph attack (FinBERT): 99.1% of headlines (5,957/6,012) fail stock-name association; 100% of attacked days show a decision change; mean delta-CR -3.67 pp; mean |delta-CR| 4.46 pp; worst case -17.70 pp (TSLA, 28 Feb 2024); best case +4.58 pp (NVDA, 29 Feb 2024)
Homoglyph single-stock (NVDA) all-days: avg attacked CR ~17.5% vs 19.22% baseline = ~1.7 pp drop ~= $17,200 loss on $1M
Hidden-text attack (FinBERT): 65.6% of headlines flip sentiment polarity; 77.3% of trials reduce CR; mean effect -3.18 pp; worst case -17.7 pp (TSLA, 28 Feb 2024)
Hidden-text single-stock (NVDA) all-days: avg attacked CR ~16.0% = ~3.2 pp drop ~= $32,200 shortfall on $1M
Clean-run CR by LLM: FinLLaMA 19.5%, FinBERT 19.2%, FinGPT 17.0%, O3 16.8%, O3 Pro 16.5%, 4o 16.3%, 4o-mini-high 16.1%, 4o-mini 15.9%, GPT-5 15.8%, Gemini Pro 1.5 15.2%
Homoglyph mapping-accuracy drop (clean->attack): FinBERT 90.04%->0.90% (-89.14 pp, worst); O3 96.72%->88.40% (-8.32 pp, most robust); FinGPT -80.41 pp; FinLLaMA -79.70 pp; Gemini Pro 1.5 -79.22 pp
System-level homoglyph impact: O3 8.3% days impacted / mean |delta-CR| 0.5 pp / worst -2.0 pp; FinBERT 100% days / 4.46 pp / -17.70 pp worst
Hidden-text flip rate: O3 lowest at 40.0% (but -0.90 avg sentiment shift when flipped); GPT-5 43.0%; O3 Pro 86.4%; most finance/general models exceed 80%
Homoglyph sanitization plugin: always defuses the Cyrillic homoglyph attack with <0.1s per-headline overhead on COTS hardware

Survey: 71% of practitioners say LLM news-driven trading is 'somewhat/common' (22%+22%) plus 26% 'very common'; 55.6% find the attack scenario 'somewhat realistic', 33% 'very realistic', only 7% 'not realistic'
baselines_compared: LSTM-only ATS vs LSTM+LLM (FinBERT) hybrid ATS (ablation/sanity check showing the LLM adds non-redundant profit: 7.9% -> 19.22% CR)
Prior open-source LSTM-only price-driven ATS from reference [21] (the authors' hybrid ATS is shown to outperform it)
Paired clean vs attacked backtests under identical market data, costs, and execution rules (delta-CR relative to paired clean run)
Cross-model comparison of 10 LLMs (FinBERT, FinGPT, FinLLaMA, O3, O3 Pro, 4o, 4o-mini-high, 4o-mini, GPT-5, Gemini Pro 1.5) for both clean CR and robustness
